aliases: Soya Beans / Soybeans (raw, dried, whole, yellow)

english: Soya Beans / Soybeans (raw, dried, whole, yellow)

food_subgroup: Soybeans (Glycine max), dried whole, yellow variety, raw

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of soybeans is approximately 50g (about 3 tablespoons of dried beans), which expands significantly upon soaking and cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Soybeans, mature seeds, raw — USDA SR Legacy, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for soybeans, mature seeds, raw. All macronutrients, micronutrients, and vitamins are exact matches from this approved primary source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; boiled soybeans GI approximately 15
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 15 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled soybeans. Raw dried soybeans do not have a directly measured GI as they are not consumed in raw dried form; the boiled soybean GI is the standard reference value applied. Glycemic load per 50g dry serving calculated as (15 × 30.2 × 0.50) / 100 = 2.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally high potassium (1797mg/100g) and very low sodium (2mg/100g) with high magnesium (280mg) and calcium (277mg)
hypertension.guidance: Soybeans are among the most potassium-rich legumes (1797mg/100g dry), with a potassium-to-sodium ratio exceeding 800:1, making them highly favourable for blood pressure management; soy isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) may additionally support endothelial function and modest blood pressure reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low glycemic index (~15 boiled) combined with very high protein (36.5g) and healthy fat (19.9g) content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Soybeans have one of the lowest GI values of any food (~15 when boiled), and their very high protein and polyunsaturated fat content further attenuate postprandial glucose response; soy isoflavones may also improve insulin sensitivity, making soybeans an excellent legume choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High polyunsaturated fat (linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids), zero cholesterol, high fibre (9.3g), and soy isoflavone content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Soybeans provide predominantly polyunsaturated fats (approximately 11.3g/100g), zero dietary cholesterol, and 9.3g dietary fibre per 100g dry weight; regular soy consumption is associated with modest LDL cholesterol reduction and cardiovascular risk reduction, supported by soy isoflavones with antioxidant and anti-atherogenic properties.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High magnesium (280mg), calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), phosphorus (704mg), zinc (4.9mg) and weak anti-inflammatory isoflavones
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Soybeans provide exceptional mineral density relevant to bone and joint health, and soy isoflavones have demonstrated weak anti-inflammatory properties that may offer modest benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soybeans (Glycine max) are the foundational legume of East and Southeast Asian cuisines, originating in China and cultivated for over 5,000 years. Raw dried yellow soybeans are rarely consumed directly in their dried state — they are almost universally processed before eating: pressed for soy milk (then coagulated into tofu), fermented into tempeh, miso, natto, and soy sauce, or extruded into textured soy protein (TSP/TVP). Nutritionally extraordinary among legumes: 36.5g protein per 100g dry weight (the highest of any common legume), 19.9g fat (predominantly polyunsaturated — linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids), and 30.2g carbohydrate with an extremely low glycemic index of approximately 15 when boiled. Very high in calcium (277mg), iron (15.7mg), potassium (1797mg), magnesium (280mg), phosphorus (704mg), and folate (375mcg). Contains isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) — phytoestrogens with modest cardiovascular, anti-inflammatory, and potentially cancer-protective properties. Cholesterol-free and suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets. The reference nutritional record for tofu, soy milk, tempeh, miso, natto, and all soy-based derivative products. A 50g dry serving provides approximately 223 kcal, 18.3g protein, and 9.95g fat.

confidence_reason: All nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (exact match: soybeans, mature seeds, raw), an approved primary source. GI value referenced from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled soybeans. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) are verified and defensible. No estimation or interpolation required for core macros or secondary nutrients.
notes: Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — soybeans are a plant food and naturally contain no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — soybeans are a plant food and naturally contain no vitamin B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — soybeans contain negligible vitamin D. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Monounsaturated_fat_g (4.4g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (11.3g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fatty acid profile for raw soybeans. Water_g (8.5g) is sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dried soybeans. GI value of 15 applies to boiled soybeans per University of Sydney GI Database; raw dried soybeans are not consumed as-is and have no directly measured GI. Glycemic load per 50g dry serving = (15 × 30.2 × 0.50) / 100 = 2.3. Hindi local name not included as soybeans are not a traditional Hindi-named staple with a widely standardised Hindi script form distinct from transliteration; this is documented as a gap. This record serves as the canonical nutritional reference for all soy-derived products including tofu, tempeh, soy milk, miso, and natto.
